MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 179 WATER RESOURCES [ESRS E3] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E3-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics Eni’s position on water • Commitment to the fight against climate change, including innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impact of Eni’s operations, also through the protection of water resources.

• Commitment to setting targets to minimise freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas(a), by seeking improved solutions or by reducing consumption through efficiency activities and by looking for solutions outside the Company and its operational boundary.

• Commitment to periodically carry out a water risk assessment, analysing the specific areas where its operational assets are located, adopting an integrated and cross‑cutting approach that involves specialist functions and business lines.

• Commitment to identifying partnerships for optimal water management in line with local needs, to promote social development and safeguard ecosystems, providing for the active involvement of stakeholders in its procedures.

Internal Regulatory System • Water resource management model defining the procedures for: (i) identifying water‑stressed areas; (ii) managing water withdrawals, use and discharges; (iii) identifying priority sites and related interventions; (iv) reporting and communication activities.
These tools aim to identify withdrawals and consumption across all business sectors, to assess and minimise potential impacts on ecosystems and communities.
The treatment, disposal or reinjection of water is governed by sector‑specific best practices.
• Actions to be implemented through the application of water risk mitigation criteria: avoid, replace, reduce, recycle, replenish.

(a) Water stressed areas are identified using Aqueduct, a tool developed by the World Resources Institute, and are monitored annually through an internal analysis carried out down to the level of the individual operational site.
Areas are classified as water‑stressed when their Baseline water stress value exceeds 40% (that is, when withdrawals of renewable water are greater than 40% of the basin’s recharge capacity).
Over the years, this journey has included its endorsement of the CEO Water Mandate and the publication of Eni’s position on water.
In 2024, Eni announced its ambition to achieve water positivity74 by 2050 in its operated sites.
This ambition is framed within an approach that also considers measures at river basin level and is inspired by the Positive Water Impact (PWI) principles set out by the CEO Water Mandate.

Actions to safeguard water will focus on the aspects identified as most critical for each territory in terms of water ‘availability’, ‘quality’ and ‘accessibility’, which represent the three dimensions to be considered under the approach proposed by the CEO Water Mandate.
Interventions will therefore be tailored to the identified needs and to the relevance of each operational site, giving priority to those located in highly water‑stressed basins.

As an intermediate milestone along the pathway towards the 2050 ambition, Eni is committed to achieving water positivity by 2035 in at least 30% of its sites that withdraw more than 0.5 Mm³/year of freshwater in areas identified as water‑stressed, based on 2023 data (priority sites)75.
This commitment therefore entails identifying actions to safeguard water resources, whose implementation and benefits must be monitored over time.
During 2024, Eni conducted a pilot study at one of its operated sites to assess the applicability of the PWI approach to the Oil & Gas sector.
In 2025, three priority sites were analysed, identifying impacts and opportunities in line with PWI principles, for which feasibility assessments will now be carried out.

Progress is monitored every six months through the HSE review process and by using a set of metrics (both those reported in the Sustainability Statement and additional indicators monitored internally), ensuring timely corrective measures in the event of deviations from expected performance.

(74) Ensuring that, at river basin level, water stewardship initiatives generate greater benefits than the impacts associated with the presence of an operational site, such as those related to the withdrawals required for industrial processes or to the quality of water returned to the environment, in line with the Guidance issued by the (75) Priority sites (with withdrawals above 0.5 Mm³ in 2023), which account for over 90% of Eni’s high‑quality freshwater operated withdrawals in waterstressed areas in 2023 (baseline).

Across industrial operations, around 86% of the water used is seawater, while the remaining 14% is freshwater, a resource that is difficult to replace in many applications and whose withdrawal may affect local availability for people, fauna and flora.

Freshwater withdrawals, mainly used for cooling, for producing demineralised water and for generating steam as an energy carrier, are attributable for approximately 80% to petrochemical and refining activities.
Seawater, on the other hand, is primarily used for cooling and, in Upstream operations, to increase the amount of crude oil that can be extracted from reservoirs (Enhanced Oil Recovery ‑ EOR).
It is also used for drilling activities, for which surface or underground brackish water can likewise be used.

The assessment of exposure to water risks at asset level, as well as of related opportunities, is carried out on an annual basis as part of the responsible water management system (see Actions taken on material IROs), taking into account the dependence of certain assets on water resources (see Biodiversity).

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E3-2] Eni recognises the importance of responsible water management and therefore closely monitors water withdrawals, discharges and consumption across all its operations, also considering the interest that all stakeholder categories76 have in this resource.

Eni’s water resource management model is based on identifying, assessing and minimising impacts on water resources, preventing adverse and/or unlawful environmental events and supporting the protection and enhancement of ecosystems.
This process is an integral part of Eni’s broader environmental management approach across its operational contexts, in which all relevant projects are subject to ESHIA77.

Each year, Eni carries out – particularly with regard to freshwater – an analysis to assess the level exposure to water risk78 of its assets and to identify opportunities to improve water management through the planning of interventions, prioritised in line with each business activity.

Eni also conducts regular supplier assessments through continuous monitoring of their ESG performance, including aspects related to water management, promoting the adoption of management systems aligned with leading international standards.
Finally, Eni’s commitment to increasing the share of produced water79 reinjected into reservoirs helps reduce withdrawals of saline or brackish water and enhances hydrocarbon recovery.

Based on the results of water risk analyses, the main improvement actions are directed and planned at the most significant sites in terms of freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas, namely downstream industrial sites located in central and southern Italy and Upstream assets in North Africa.

Eni implements actions to safeguard water resources through two main strategies: on the one hand, improving efficiency through internal recycling of freshwater; on the other, replacing high‑quality80 freshwater sources with lower‑quality alternatives.

The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts identified for freshwater consumption are presented below, divided into: (i) water stress areas; and (ii) areas not at water stress.
Water stress areas Eni implements actions to safeguard water resources in water‑stresse areas.

Below are some examples of measures aimed at replacing high‑quality freshwater with the following types of water: • wastewater, consisting of a combination of civil and industrial discharges as well as rainfall collected and drained through sewer networks or drainage systems.

Some examples of actions carried out, in Italian sites, during the year include: (i) Ravenna petrochemical hub, with a wastewater reuse plant, operational from 2026; (ii) Brindisi petrochemical site, with a plant for the reuse of approximately 0.4 Mm³ of wastewater per year, expected to be operational by 202681; (iii) Gela biorefinery, where the reuse of urban wastewater for industrial purposes has been increased since August 2024; (76) For further information on community engagement, see (77) Environmental, Social and Health Impact Assessments.

More than half of the total expenditure for water resource management in 2025 is allocated to interventions at sites located in water‑stressed areas.
For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €0.7 billion for water resource management.

In 2025, water withdrawals decreased compared to 2024 (‑341 Mm³), driven by reductions in both seawater and freshwater withdrawals.

Seawater withdrawals, amounting to 707 Mm³ (86% of total water withdrawals), fell by 326 Mm³ year on year, mainly due to shutdowns at the Italian petrochemical plants in Brindisi and Priolo (which together account for more than 60% of the reduction) and to lower production at the Enipower Brindisi power plant (where withdrawals decreased by more than 111 Mm³).
Freshwater withdrawals in 2025, representing 14% of total water withdrawals and attributable for approximately 80% to petrochemical and refining activities, fell by more than 10% compared with 2024.

This reduction is mainly due to Versalis, totalling ‑11 Mm³, of which 8 Mm³ relate to the Mantua site, in Italy (thanks to optimised water consumption enabled by operation at reduced loads) and 1.3 Mm³ to Porto Marghera (Italy), following changes in the production setup.

Further contributions to the reduction in freshwater withdrawals were provided by the Sannazzaro refinery (more than ‑0.6 Mm³), in Italy, as a result of the stable operation of the Water Reuse plant, the divestment of onshore sites in Nigeria at the end of 2024 and the increased internal reuse of treated groundwater (at Eni Rewind sites).
The volumes of recycled freshwater, more than 72% of which are attributable to Versalis, decreased by 5% compared to 2024, mainly due to the general shutdown of the Ragusa plant (Italy).
The percentage of Eni’s freshwater reuse in 2025 remained stable at 90%.
In 2025, total water consumption in water stress areas accounted for 40% of Eni’s overall consumption.

It is specified that high‑quality freshwater withdrawals – sourced from surface water, groundwater and municipal water supply – carried out in these areas represented 2% of Eni’s total water withdrawals.

The percentage of produced water reinjection increased to 56% in 2025 (51% in 2024), reflecting both higher reinjected volumes in Turkmenistan, Mexico and Italy, and lower the overall volumes of produced water generated during the period.

Eni Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (BES) Policy • Technical guidelines for biodiversity risk assessment and management across global activities and decision‑making processes throughout the project lifecycle(a), considering potential impacts on local communities, where relevant.
• Adoption of the mitigation hierarchy(b) to manage identified impacts, prioritising preventive over corrective measures to avoid net biodiversity loss or, where feasible, achieving a net gain.
• Commitments adopted over time for the protection of areas of ecological significance.
Eni Position on Water • Close link between water resources, biodiversity and ecosystem services protection, and forest conservation.

Eni No-Go Commitment Eni Position on Biomass • Formal commitment not to perform Oil & Gas exploration and development activities within the boundaries of Natural Sites included in the UNESCO World Heritage List(c).

• General principles to ensure the sustainability of agricultural practices, sourcing and consumption of raw materials, including: traceability and transparency along the supply chain; selection of suppliers meeting ESG criteria; and the inclusion of contractual clauses to ensure the sourcing of certified biomass(d) only, not originated from land converted from areas of high biodiversity value or from ecosystems providing essential ecological services, such as carbon capture and storage.
Internal Regulatory System • Processes for identifying, prioritising, managing, and monitoring biodiversity impacts and actions, in accordance with the HSE Integrated Management System.
(a) In joint ventures in which Eni is not the operator, it is committed to promoting with its partners the development and adoption of management best practices in line with the BES Policy.

(b) The mitigation hierarchy is an internationally recognised best practice for managing environmental risks and potential impacts, based on a sequence of actions: (i) anticipate and avoid impacts; (ii) minimize impacts where avoidance is not possible; (iii) restore; and (iv) offset.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E4-4] While Eni has not established consolidated quantitative targets, it remains committed to implementing actions aimed at safeguarding biodiversity and ecosystem services by focusing on the prevention and minimisation of risks and impacts.
Biodiversity is site‑specific and characterised by unique features that vary significantly across geographical areas, environmental conditions of ecosystems, and ecological interactions.
The lack of a universally recognised metric for measuring global biodiversity makes it complex to define aggregated targets at the Group level.

For this reason, Eni applies a ‘site‑specific’ management approach, implementing, where necessary, environmental management plans that identify targeted measures and local indicators, as described below.
This approach allows the specific features of each environmental context to be addressed more effectively, ensuring that actions are concrete, measurable, and tailored to impacts on the territory.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS and OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3] Impacts identification process The process of identification and assessment of dependencies and impacts on biodiversity and ecosystem services (BES) related to Eni’s activities82 is integrated into the HSE Management System.
Each site, in line with ISO 14001:2015 certification, conducts studies to identify and evaluate environmental impacts (EIA ‑ Environmental Impact Assessment).

In addition, Eni annually maps its sites and concessions to verify proximity to legally protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs), and to assess the potential presence of priority species from the IUCN Red List83, in order to ensure more effective management of impacts in priority conservation areas.

The screening identifies the sites with the (82) For any other impacts on the value chain, see (83) The IUCN Red List is the global database developed by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) that classifies the conservation status of species according to internationally recognized scientific criteria.

The geographic distribution (range) of species is available through IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool), a platform that integrates global data on species, protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas.

184 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX highest risk of biodiversity loss, especially those overlapping with at least one important biodiversity area, where further in‑depth studies (BES Assessments) are carried out, following a risk‑based prioritization, to verify the possible presence of significant unmanaged residual impacts.

During the impact assessment process, related to EIA studies or BES assessments, Eni evaluates the possible interactions between company activities and the environment, considering the main global drivers of biodiversity loss, such as land and sea‑use change, overexploitation of natural resources, the global phenomenon of climate change, pollution, and the introduction of invasive species.
This analysis defines the operational and environmental context, identifies sensitive BES receptors, and assesses main dependencies and both direct84 and indirect85 impacts.

The significance of biodiversity impacts is determined by combining the magnitude of the impact (e.g., pressures generated by the project on environmental matrices) with the sensitivity of the BES receptor involved (e.g., presence of species at risk of extinction), and assigning a significance category.
If significant residual impacts are identified, they are managed through specific measures included in the site environmental management plans or through dedicated Biodiversity Action Plans (BAPs).

Consultation and collaboration with communities, indigenous peoples, and other local stakeholders help to understand expectations and concerns, determine how ecosystem services and biodiversity are used, and develop management options that address local needs.

Material impacts In the absence of actions targeted at mitigation, Eni’s activities can cause significant negative impacts in terms of degradation or loss of biodiversity (habitats, ecosystems, and species) and of ecosystem services.
The extent of these impacts depends on project complexity, the value of the natural environment, and the social context of reference.

Among the most common impacts are those associated with changes in land (or sea) use and with the physical presence of plants and infrastructure, which can result in habitat removal, degradation, or fragmentation, with consequences for the distribution and abundance of species.

In Oil & Gas Upstream activities and in the large‑ scale development of plants for renewable energy generation, such impacts are particularly relevant when they involve natural or semi‑ natural areas86.
Moreover, wind plants can have negative impacts on vulnerable species, such as birds of prey, due to the risk of collision with turbines, wind blades, and distribution lines.

Downstream activities instead develop predominantly in contexts already industrialized; therefore, the contribution to changes in land use is generally limited and does not represent to date a material impact in terms of loss of biodiversity.

Material risks and opportunities [E4-1] The Process and results of the double materiality assessment did not identify material risks (including systemic risks)87 or currently realised opportunities related to biodiversity.

Nevertheless, Eni continues to monitor potential risks and opportunities by consulting publicly available scenarios, used to understand how changes in the state of nature may translate into physical or transition risks, as well as into opportunities, including those associated with nature‑based solutions.
Physical risks are associated with ecosystem degradation and potential reduction of natural resources, which may affect the operability and profitability of assets.

Transition risks, instead, derive from the evolution of the regulatory framework, environmental protection policies, and stakeholder expectations, which may result in limitations on access to natural resources or land use in certain geographical areas.
Within this scope are also included reputational risks, connected to the perception of the energy sector, which could affect litigation, reputation and access to financing.
The analysis was aimed in particular at exploring transition risks linked to key dependencies on nature.

Regarding physical risks, water represents the main dependency for some assets, as it is essential in production processes; the metrics on water withdrawals and use are reported in the Water resources chapter, while resilience to physical risks associated with reduced water availability (water stress, drought) is analysed as part of the assessment of asset resilience to climate‑related physical risks, in Climate Change chapter.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 185 other dependencies on natural resources, analysed in terms of transition risks, varies by business type (for example, biomass sourcing is particularly significant for biofuel production).

In this context, the resilience analysis, conducted in 2024, focused on Eni’s direct operations and made reference to a publicly available scenario developed by ‘Inevitable Policy Response’ (Forecast Policy Scenario ‑ FPS89), to explore possible future trajectories.

The scenario, developed over the 2020‑2050 time horizon, highlights a global context characterized by a growing awareness of the importance of safeguarding biodiversity and ecosystem services, which could lead, through new regulations, to constraints on land availability for bioenergy crops in certain areas or for renewable energy installations.
Eni’s resilience to such risks is based on a strategy that integrates global portfolio diversification, the development of new technologies, and the adoption of circular business models.

In the Agribusiness context, for example, the company has implemented an approach that diversifies the type of agri‑feedstock used and sourcing regions, and it valorises waste and residues to reduce reliance on virgin raw materials.

For Plenitude wind farms, starting from 2025 reporting year, a methodological update has been introduced to improve the spatial representation of assets and the consistency of overlap and adjacency analyses.
Under the new approach, wind turbines are modelled as curvilinear surfaces based on rotor diameter.
The wind turbines have been grouped for adjacency analysis with protected areas.

This update enables a more accurate representation of the area actually occupied by the turbines, replacing the previous modelling based on broader wind farm area boundaries; this resulted in a downward revision of surface values compared to the previous reporting period.

For the sites overlapping protected areas or KBAs, and for Upstream concessions overlapping with operational activities within the overlap area, Eni initiates BES Assessment studies, according to a risk‑based prioritisation approach and, where necessary, defines a Biodiversity Action Plan (BAP) to manage significant residual impacts on such areas.
BAPs can also be developed for sites not overlapping with protected areas or KBAs, if required by EIA studies, financing or permitting conditions, or other management needs.

The main impact driver identified for Eni’s activities relates to land use change resulting from Oil & Gas Upstream infrastructures (for example pipeline installation and well pad construction) and from Plenitude’s electricity generation sites.
Such changes may entail habitat loss or degradation, with potential disturbances to species.

To mitigate this impact, the BAPs focus on two priority areas of intervention: (i) the restoration of natural habitats that have been modified or degraded; and (ii) monitoring campaigns aimed at confirming the presence of species at risk and assessing potential impacts of activities on their conservation status at the local level.

RESOURCE USE AND CIRCULAR ECONOMY [ESRS E5] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E5-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Commitment to the promotion of development models based on the regenerative principles of the circular economy that minimize the use of virgin resources and reduce waste, maximizing the recovery and valorisation of waste and residues.

Internal Regulatory System • Implementing actions across its operations aimed to improve efficiency, reduce waste, maximise the recovery and valorisation of waste and by‑products, use secondary raw materials or renewable sources, extend the useful life of assets and innovate processes and products, with the broader goal of creating long‑term value for both the environment and society.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E5-3] Although Eni has not adopted quantitative targets, aside from the business target relating to biorefining capacity outlined in the Strategy section, the Company promotes the prevention of waste generation in line with the priority criteria established under EU legislation and ensures proper waste management in accordance with its Internal Regulatory System.
Eni is also consistently engaged in implementing projects with a strong circular footprint (see Actions taken on material IROs).

To guide these processes, the Company adopts an Internal Regulatory System and an HSE Management System that set out operational guidelines for all business areas, informed by the operating context, environmental compliance requirements, stakeholder expectations, and the assessment of impacts, risks and opportunities.

(93) Also recognized as a Ramsar site and a WHS site (UNESCO World Heritage Site).

By their nature, Eni’s operations entail the production of waste, whose negative effects include: (i) the potential contamination of environmental matrices (soil, water and air) in cases of inadequate management, with possible consequences for local communities; (ii) impacts associated with the transport and treatment of waste at destination facilities; and (iii) land use associated to such facilities.
Waste generated by Eni originates both from production activities and from the remediation of contaminated soil and groundwater.

Remediation waste mainly consists of excavated soil and rock, demolition materials and groundwater; the latter, which in 2025 accounted for around 60% of total remediation waste, is treated in specific Groundwater Treatment Plants and is usually contaminated by hydrocarbons, benzene and dichloroethane.
The production of remediation waste is also linked to assets acquired by Eni from other companies, where Eni has never directly operated.

With regard to waste from production activities, produced water from Upstream operations, when not managed as discharge, represents the most significant contribution (approximately one‑third of total waste from production activities).
Produced water typically has very high salinity and variable composition, with residual components such as hydrocarbons and additives resulting from the separation process.

At the same time, the materiality assessment has also identified a positive environmental impact associated with circular economy actions, including the conversion and redevelopment of decommissioned assets and sites, as well as the use of renewable and recycled raw materials in production processes.

Circular economy initiatives are implemented across the Group’s business lines, generating market opportunities along the value chain and contributing to the evolution of its industrial models.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E5-2] The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified are presented below and organised into two areas: (i) circular economy, for which the actions are described according to the different types of circularity interventions (extension of useful asset life, asset conversion and redevelopment; use of circular raw materials and alternative feedstocks; recycling and recovery technologies); (ii) waste.

Circular Economy Eni integrates circular economy principles into its business model, applying them both to the development of new product value chains and to existing ones, with the aim of improving efficiency in the use of resources and extending the lifecycle of assets and products.
Below is an overview of the main actions, organised by type of circular economy intervention.

The main resources allocated during the year to activities related to biorefineries and bio‑feedstock production amounted to €0.8 billion, while those dedicated to other circular economy initiatives totalled €0.1 billion.
For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €7 billion to biorefining/bio‑feedstock activities and €0.8 billion to other circular economy initiatives.

To support its initiatives, Eni has developed, together with the Scuola Superiore Sant’Anna, a model and tool for measuring circularity, incorporating the requirements of the UNI 11820 and ISO 59020 standards.

In line with its commitment to transparency and accountability, Eni has also obtained third‑party verification of its level of circularity under UNI 11820, both for support functions (business‑support processes and services provided at headquarters and local offices in Italy) and for the Gela biorefinery (in Sicily), thereby initiating a structured path of continuous analysis and improvement of its circularity performance.

EXTENSION OF USEFUL ASSET LIFE, ASSET CONVERSION AND REDEVELOPMENT Extending asset useful life, converting and redeveloping existing infrastructure represents a key lever of Eni’s circular economy approach, aimed at maximising the value of existing facilities throughout their lifecycle while reducing the consumption of new resources.
Within this framework, the transformation of traditional refineries into biorefineries plays a central role.

The conversion of the Livorno site forms part of the Group’s broader plan to expand its biorefining capacity from the current 1.65 million tonnes per year to more than 5 million tonnes per year by 2030.

For example, in collaboration with other companies of the Group, remediated industrial areas have been redeveloped as sites for renewable energy production, combining environmental remediation with new energy generation capacity within a single territorial‑revitalisation strategy.

In this context, from 2025 a new Plenitude photovoltaic plant in Porto Marghera has become operational, complementing similar initiatives already implemented in Italy, in Porto Marghera, Assemini, Gela, Porto Torres, Priolo and Ponticelle.

In the Upstream business, circular economy principles are also being applied to the valorisation of mature assets, including assessments of the feasibility of converting offshore platforms in the Adriatic and Mediterranean Seas into infrastructure for subsea data centres, or converting Italian industrial sites into areas dedicated to renewable energy, logistics or data centres.

USE OF CIRCULAR RAW MATERIALS AND ALTERNATIVE FEEDSTOCKS The use of circular raw materials and alternative feedstocks plays a key role in reducing reliance on virgin resources and enabling materials to re‑enter production cycles, thereby contributing to the closure of their lifecycle.

This area encompasses biorefining initiatives, the production of advanced biofuels and the valorisation of residues and by‑products, as well as the development of industrial solutions aimed at expanding the use of circular materials across a wide range of applications.

Key initiatives include the production of advanced biofuels obtained mainly from residual feedstocks, such as used cooking oil, and the production of biomethane derived from organic residues, including agricultural and agro‑industrial by‑products, livestock effluents and organic waste.

For biofuels production, a residual share is sourced from vegetable oils derived from the cultivation of oilseed crops through certified supply chains which include programmes in various Countries in Africa, South‑East Asia and Central Asia, aimed at restoring degraded land and ensuring crop rotation in line with the EU’s RED Directive.
By‑products are also valorised and converted into animal feed and fertilisers, strengthening the circular value chain approach.

In the chemicals sector, Versalis aims to diversify feedstock sources using raw materials from renewable sources, such as biomass, and through the development of products derived from secondary raw materials from recycling94.

Additional circular solutions currently under development include residual materials for road‑infrastructure applications, recycled plastics or alternative raw materials in furniture production, and the use of recovered post‑industrial residues.

RECYCLING AND RECOVERY TECHNOLOGIES Initiatives in this area focus on promoting material recovery and the reintegration of waste into production cycles through the adoption of innovative recycling and valorisation technologies.

In the chemicals sector, investments are being directed towards the development of recycled materials and a range of technologies for the recycling of plastic and rubber, including through collaborations with partners across the value chain.
New facilities have been launched to convert plastic waste into secondary raw materials that can be reused in production processes.

At the same time, agreements have been established with other operators to strengthen an integrated recycling system, enabling the recovery and valorisation of different types of plastic waste and their transformation into new materials or products.
In the remediation sector, Eni Rewind is developing solutions aimed at the recovery and reuse of waste and contaminated soils.
In this context, new platforms dedicated to waste treatment and soil recovery are currently under development at the Ponticelle site (in Italy), with start‑up expected in 2026.

Further technologies capable of converting hard‑to‑recycle waste into new products, such as fuels, are also being assessed, and research projects are under way to further enhance recycling solutions in other sectors, including renewable energy.

Waste Across all its operational contexts, in addition to complying with applicable waste‑management regulations, Eni is committed to reducing the environmental impacts associated with the various phases of the waste‑management process.

The main actions in this area are based on the following principles set out in Eni’s Internal Regulatory System: • Sustainable operational waste management, inspired by the waste‑hierarchy model established by European legislation.
This model aims to prevent waste generation and prioritises recovery solutions over disposal.

In Italy, for example, through Eni Rewind’s brokerage activities, technically feasible solutions are selected based on the characteristics of the waste, giving priority to: (i) recovery operations; (ii) treatments that reduce the quantities sent for disposal; and (iii) the selection of suitable plants as close as possible to the production site; • Verification of third‑party reliability, ensured through targeted checks on the parties involved along the value chain and through periodic supplier assessments carried out by specialist technical units (e.g.

In particular, in Italy and wherever required by current legislation, Eni relies exclusively on authorised operators; (94) Secondary raw materials are recycled materials that can be used in production processes either in place of, or alongside, virgin raw materials.

In 2025, more than 4 million tonnes of waste were generated at Eni, of which 1.5 million tonnes from production activities and 3.1 million tonnes from remediation activities, showing an overall increasing trend compared to 2024.
Waste from production activities increased during the year for both hazardous and non‑hazardous waste.

This trend was mainly driven by the increase in produced water at Eni Mediterranea Idrocarburi sites, in Sicily, the disposal of wastewater as waste at the Calenzano fuel depot (following the shutdown of the site water treatment plant due to the accidental event of December 2024), the volumes generated at the Livorno refinery in connection with the conversion of the site into a biorefinery, and those generated by construction works at the Porto Marghera biorefinery related to the construction of new plants (Steam Reforming Unit, Biojet section and Ecofining Deoxygenation section).
In 2025, almost 370 thousand tonnes of waste from production activities were sent for recovery and recycling, an increase compared to 2024.
Waste from remediation activities during the same period amounted to 3.1 million tonnes (of which more than 2.5 million tonnes were attributable to Eni Rewind), a figure decreasing compared to 2024.

The downward trend is mainly attributable: (i) the start‑up of the groundwater treatment plant at the Pomezia fuel depot, in Italy, which allowed the management of extracted groundwater as waste to be eliminated; (ii) the completion of remediation activities at the Eni Rewind sites of Avenza and Brindisi, in Italy; (iii) the reduction of activities at the Eni Rewind site of Manfredonia, in Italy, with a consequent reduction in the volumes of groundwater treated and managed as waste.
In terms of composition, the majority of remediation waste consists of groundwater treated in Groundwater Treatment Plants (almost 60% in 2025), partly reused and partly returned to the environment.
In 2025, more than 638 thousand tonnes of remediation waste were sent for recovery.

228 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Initiatives for vulnerable customers138 Following the end of the regulated gas market in Italy in December 2023, Plenitude also confirmed for 2025 a range of offers with characteristics and price levels similar to those of the former regulated regime.
The initiative provides benefits on the supply of gas and electricity from renewable sources covered by Guarantees of Origin, as well as a promotional offer on boilers and air conditioning systems.

Customer engagement in the energy transition With regard to activities related to efficient energy usage, Plenitude is committed to guiding customers towards energy awareness by providing personalised advice based on their energy profile, through a dedicated web area and app reserved to customers.

To this end, also in 2025, Plenitude made available to its customers a tool enabling them to estimate the electricity production of a residential photovoltaic system, ensuring, at the offer evaluation stage, the possibility to estimate potential savings based on customers’ consumption habits.

The ‘Plenitude Insieme’ loyalty programme, in addition to building a lasting and valuable relationship with customers, offers initiatives aimed at increasing awareness and knowledge of energy efficiency.
In 2025, the participation rate of registered users remained high (over 80%), confirming the continued engagement of customers in the energy transition journey.

In 2025, in fact, 83% of enrolled customers interacted with the programme at least once, and more than 390,000 people deepened their knowledge of energy efficiency (an increase of 95% compared to 2024).

In 2025, Plenitude launched the ‘Adopt a Panel’ service, enabling residential customers to virtually consume solar energy produced in real time by photovoltaic panels located at Plenitude-owned plants, without the need to install a domestic system.
The service allows customers to benefit from a 100% discount on electricity charges for the energy produced by the panel and consumed by the customer at the same time.
It also enables customers to monitor energy production and receive energy efficiency advice through the Plenitude app or their personal web area.

In this context, Eni contributes its experience as an international energy company to the development of policies and regulations aimed at supporting the Net Zero transition, taking into account the social, economic and environmental dimensions of the contexts in which it operates.

The initiative, which implements the principle underlying the SDG 17 of the United Nations 2030 Agenda, aims to foster the growth of the corporate ecosystem and the progressive development of distinctive skills in the field of Risk & Control Governance through a collaborative approach involving partners, suppliers and stakeholders.

In particular, Eni contributes to the development of strategies and regulations aimed at accelerating the transition to Net Zero, clearly and transparently conveying its positioning on climate change and related strategic topics.
Eni recognizes the value of active participation in business associations’ work to develop and share best practices and advocacy positions aimed at promoting the energy transition.

In this regard, Eni proactively commits to periodically addressing the positions of each association, particularly those whose positions diverge from Eni’s Climate Advocacy Principles, towards an approach consistent with the need to act effectively to tackle climate change.

Furthermore, every public position submitted to stakeholders and USG regulatory bodies (e.g., SEC, BOEM - Bureau of Ocean Energy Management) is published on the relevant websites of those stakeholders and regulatory bodies.
TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S2-5] In line with its medium-to-long-term sustainable supply chain strategy, Eni has established specific targets for supplier management and procurement.
These targets aim to ensure that ESG-related requirements and safeguards for suppliers serve as a driver of sustainable development across the entire supply chain.
(c) Ratio between the total number of strategic suppliers analysed based on their ESG positioning and the total number of strategic suppliers (i.e.
The target has been extended to 2026 following the achievement of the 2025 target.

This approach seeks to engage all levels of the supply chain in a path of sustainable growth through the promotion of sustainability principles, promoting environmental and social sustainability principles, to raise awareness and fostering more responsible business practices.

In an industrial landscape increasingly focused on sustainability, Eni strengthens its leadership through the Open- es initiative, a digital platform and system alliance aimed at building a more resilient supply chain and supporting a modern, competitive entrepreneurial ecosystem aligned with sustainable transition objectives that constitute a central pillar of Eni’s strategy.

By adopting a sustainable supply chain management strategy, Eni promotes more sustainable and responsible behaviours, transparency and innovation among suppliers, reducing potential inefficiencies and enhancing environmental, social and governance standards across the entire procurement cycle.

This approach generates tangible value for both Eni — through more competitive, resilient partners fully aligned with corporate objectives — and stakeholders, who benefit from a more robust, inclusive and energy-transition-oriented productive ecosystem.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S2 SBM-3; S2-4; G1-2] The sustainable supply chain management strategy of Eni is based on the sharing of values, commitments and objectives with its supply chain and is articulated around three pillars, on which specific actions are implemented: • systemic and inclusive approach, involving the supply chain in a path of improvement and sustainable development, through common objectives and differentiated models depending on the ESG maturity of companies.

In this context, Eni promotes Open-es, launched with Boston Consulting Group and Google Cloud in 2021, to create a multi-stakeholder initiative among the industrial, financial and associative sectors to support companies in the process of measuring and improving their ESG performance.

To date, more than 30 partners have joined, including large industrial players, financial institutions and associations, and over 40,000 companies have registered, of which over 9,400 belong to Eni’s supply chain, both in Italy and abroad; • development and enhancement of best practices, supporting suppliers in meeting ESG requirements, providing tools for their sustainable development pathway and, more broadly, for competitiveness.

These include tools for: (i) measuring and improving ESG maturity through metrics aligned with the regulatory context and benchmarking against sector peers, with access to personalised development plans, solutions offered by specialised ESG providers and free events and training programmes; (ii) financial support through ‘Sustainable Supply Chain Finance’ (launched in 2023) to request early payment of invoices without impacts on credit lines, in order to incentivise a sustainable development pathway that integrates improved financial resource management with enhanced ESG performance.

In 2025, invoice advances were granted for a total amount of approximately €240 million; (iii) offering products and services under favourable conditions, such as solutions for energy efficiency and the use of HVOlution biofuel in transport; (iv) recognition of excellence through the Supply Chain Award, to share best practices and reward the most distinctive and innovative companies in the ESG field; (v) support for the competitiveness of the supply chain in the context of the energy transition.

In 2025, the ‘Energia di filiera (Value chain energy)’ programme was launched, a development initiative dedicated to Eni qualified suppliers, in particular SMEs, operating in the industrial areas most impacted by the transformation of Eni’s businesses, with the aim of supporting them in defining an improvement and development plan in a continuously evolving market context.

In 2025, the focus of the programme was the Versalis Transformation Plan, involving more than 200 SMEs, half of them contractors located in the Italian industrial districts of Brindisi, Priolo, and Ragusa; • pervasiveness of sustainability in the procurement process, represented by the integration of environmental protection, social growth and economic development principles at every stage.

Eni has adopted the ‘Sustainable Supply Chain Framework’, a governance mechanism that combines corporate objectives, legislative requirements, targets, and specific action plans that impact the procurement process and the supply chain more broadly.

This framework is implemented through a cross-cutting oversight of the different sustainability dimensions, with a focus on priority ESG topics periodically identified based on the corporate strategic plan and the evolution of the regulatory framework.

In 2025, for certain ESG dimensions identified as priorities for Eni — such as climate change, supply chain governance, human rights, cybersecurity and safety — verification and in-depth review activities continued, using specific minimum criteria for the evaluation of bids, in addition to dedicated standard contractual clauses.

The expenses related to the implementation of Eni’s Sustainable Supply Chain strategy are fully integrated into ordinary management and business operations and fall within the broader structural costs incurred.

The Company also aims to promote greater accountability among direct commercial partners, particularly major market players, encouraging them to regularly conduct due diligence on their third parties and actively oversee environmental and social sustainability topics throughout the entire supply chain.

Substantial contribution to climate change mitigation For the assessment of substantial contribution to climate change mitigation, criterion c) related to activity 3.17 as stated in EU Regulation 2021/2139 was applied, as follows: c) derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials, and the greenhouse gas emissions over their lifecycle are lower than the greenhouse gas emissions in the lifecycle of equivalent primary form plastics manufactured from fossil fuels.
Greenhouse gas emissions over the lifecycle are calculated using Recommendation 2013/179/EU or, alternatively, ISO 14067:2018 or ISO 14064-1:2018.
Greenhouse gas emissions quantified over the lifecycle are verified by an independent third party.
In this context, chemicals derived from hydrocarbons were identified as equivalent to resins and plastics derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials.

Do No Significant Harm (‘DNSH’) Climate change adaptation The Group has performed a risk assessment of the exposure of all aligned activities to acute and chronic weather events as required by Appendix ‘A’ to the Climate Delegated Regulation based on the Company’s methodology described herein.

The management has assessed the risk of exposure of the Group’s assets to climate-related acute and chronic hazards, following the guidelines of Appendix A to the Climate Delegated Regulation, setting generic criteria for DNSH to climate change adaptation.

The Group has put in place management control systems and procedures to identify, evaluate and mitigate physical climate risks, which the Company defines as the risk that potential perspective changes in meteorological patterns, extreme weather phenomena and gradual changes in weather conditions and in the physical environment linked to climate change may adversely and significantly affect assets’ future performance, safety of operations and future expected cash flows, so to significantly harm the objective of climate change adaptation.

The management regularly reviews the exposure of the Group’s assets to the acute and chronic climate-related hazards described in the above- mentioned Appendix A and other natural hazards based on a proprietary methodology to identify physical climate risks over a long-term horizon.

This assessment considers various timing horizons based on assets’ useful lives (about thirty years for solar installation, wind mills and biorefineries, whereas recharging points for EV have seven years of useful live).

Eni’s assessment methodology of assets’ exposure to natural hazards features the following steps: • it utilizes input data furnished by an external provider, which has elaborated detailed geographic maps of prospective climate-related risks ensuring a full coverage of onshore and offshore areas where Eni’s assets are located.

Those climate maps combine the most updated climate forecast models, also incorporating historical weather patterns, to provide expected quantitative trends in the evolution of climate- related events (like expected number of days with temperatures above or below historical averages, wind strength, rain intensity, etc.

The outcome of that review informs the management of the residual riskand: • in case of chronic climate-related hazards, monitoring activities are designed, planned, and carried out leading to the possible implementation and follow-up of remediation measures; • in case of acute climate-related hazards, asset integrity process is activated which can lead to the definition and activation of an adaptation plan.
Based on the described procedure and methodology, Eni’s installations for the production of plastics in primary form show marginal residual exposure to prospective adverse weather events.

For these risks, a site-specific risk assessment is planned during 2026 in order to define the corresponding monitoring plan and actions, with the objective of making the activity climate change adapted within the timeframe of the plan.
No violations of the DNSH principle were found in relation to the other environmental objectives.

Substantial contribution to climate change mitigation Each batch of HVO manufactured in 2025 has been reviewed to assess the substantial contribution to climate change mitigation.

Volumes of HVO manufactured using food and feed crops as feedstock have been excluded from the KPI, as well as those produced using agricultural biomass that does not comply with the criteria laid down in Article 29, paragraphs 2 to 5, of Directive (EU) 2018/2001.

The greenhouse gas emission savings from the HVO volumes manufactured from sustainable feedstock have been measured by applying the GHG saving methodology and the relative fossil fuel comparator set out in Annex V to Directive (EU) 2018/2001.
The saving has been calculated for each kind of biomass used as feedstock.
Based on the outcome of this review, 95% of the volumes marketed to third parties at the Gela biorefinery have been assessed to contribute substantially to climate change mitigation.
The activity turnover, OpEx, and CapEx have apportioned to the relevant KPIs in proportion to the percentage of environmentally sustainable manufactured volumes of HVO.

Compliance with the Minimum Safeguards (Ms) - Article 3 ‘c’ of the EU Taxonomy Regulation The criteria for the eco-sustainability of economic activities outlined in article 3 of the Taxonomy Regulation call for respecting minimum safeguards when conducting business (referred to in paragraph ‘c’).

When companies implement these procedures, they must also comply with the ‘do no significant harm’ principle outlined in Article 2, paragraph 17 of Regulation (EU) 2019/2088, the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR).

The SFDR requires financial market participants to assess the ESG risk of the investments within the financial products they intend to offer investors, measuring the ESG performance of the investee companies against a predefined set of key impact indicators in critical ‘principal adverse impact’ areas.

Five of these indicators have a social nature: (i) violations of the UN Global Compact principles and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises; (ii) lack of processes and compliance mechanisms to monitor compliance with the previous point’s principles; (iii) unadjusted gender pay gap; (iv) Board gender diversity; and (v) exposure to controversial weapons.

The definition of sustainable investment in article 2 (17) of the SFDR states that an investment is sustainable if it contributes to broadly defined environmental or social objectives, provided that it does not harm any of these objectives.
Finally, environmental protection is treated by the sustainability performance criteria set article 3 of the Taxonomy Regulation, while science/ technology are out of the scope.
In conclusion, considering the draft Report ‘Minimum Safeguards’, Eni believes it complies with the safeguard clause of Article 3, paragraph ‘c’ of the EU Taxonomy Regulation.
